pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: The constellation of unremitting, unexplained abdominal pain, profound autonomic instability (hypertension, tachycardia), acute severe psychosis, and rapidly evolving peripheral neuropathy is pathognomonic for a rare disorder of heme synthesis. Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: When questioned about the patient's new prescriptions, her husband mentioned she had started taking a low-dose medication for migraines two weeks prior to admission. She also admitted to starting a severe calorie-restricted diet to lose weight quickly. The patient was prescribed Phenobarbital (a potent CYP450 inducer) for migraines, combined with fasting (caloric restriction), which precipitated the crisis. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is caused by a genetic deficiency in Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS, previously known as PBG deaminase), an enzyme in the heme synthesis pathway. Triggers (like CYP450-inducing drugs, alcohol, infection, or severe fasting) increase the demand for heme synthesis. Because the enzyme is deficient, this increased demand leads to massive accumulation of the neurotoxic precursors delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA) and Porphobilinogen (PBG). These precursors are neurotoxic, directly causing axonal damage (peripheral neuropathy), visceral smooth muscle contraction (abdominal pain), and central nervous system dysfunction (psychosis, seizures, encephalopathy, and severe autonomic instability). Diagnosis is confirmed by massively elevated urine levels of ALA and PBG. Treatment requires IV administration of Hemin (heme arginate) to repress the synthesis pathway and rapid cessation of all trigger agents.
